title: Update Systemd security settings
body:
As part of https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Changes/SystemdSecurityHardening which has been approved for Fedora 40, I am working on updating Systemd services to add additional hardening settings, please review this PR and let me know if you have any feedback.  I have turned off both ProtectHome and ProtectSystem since the user service is just a symlink to the system service but ideally we would turn them both on and just add an allow list for specific directories to have write access.  If you have that list, please let me know and I can test it out. 

https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.exec.html has detailed information on each of these settings including the version of Systemd where they were introduced.

Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

> Podman is a container manager and basically needs all of these things. Even if some one the things may not be strictly required users expect to be able to launch containers with full privileges, if we lock down podman that would not be possible breaking that expectation.

If you think Podman would need to retain all these privileges by default including say the ability to load kernel modules and change hostnames for the system it is running in, I can close this out.

> > Podman is a container manager and basically needs all of these things. Even if some one the things may not be strictly required users expect to be able to launch containers with full privileges, if we lock down podman that would not be possible breaking that expectation.
> 
> If you think Podman would need to retain all these privileges by default including say the ability to load kernel modules and change hostnames for the system it is running in, I can close this out.

Podman itself may not need to do that but if users run a rootful container with --privileged and without uts namespace they can change the hostname, it is not like that I expect users to need that but --privileged implies no restrictions. The same for kernel modules or any other of these setting.
Also many of your settings already break basic podman usage already namesapces, setuid, NoNewPrivileges etc...

I think these setting can make sense for many units, but podman as general purpose container manager cannot be limited by default as this could break legit use cases.
